Football dashboard

The 2021/22 season in the rear-view mirror — xG team analytics, player profiles and second-order insights, frozen at full time. Matchday results and standings live on the current dashboard only.

Big five leagues 2021/22Season archiveUnderstat

Team analytics (2021/22, Understat)

Metric glossary — what xG, npxG, xGBuildup, PPDA and friends actually mean
xG — expected goals
How good a team's or player's chances were. Every shot gets a value between 0 and 1: the probability that an average player scores from that spot and situation. A tap-in is ~0.9, a hopeful 30-metre hit ~0.02. Add them up and you get how many goals the chances “should” have produced.
xA — expected assists
The same idea for passing: the xG of the shot a pass created. A player who keeps serving up big chances gets a high xA even if teammates keep missing them.
npxG / npg — non-penalty xG and goals
xG and goals with penalties removed. A penalty is worth ~0.76 xG no matter who wins it, so stripping them out shows how much a player or team creates from open play.
G−xG — finishing
Goals scored minus expected goals. Above zero: converting chances an average finisher would miss. Below zero: missing chances that usually go in. Tends to swing back toward zero over time.
KP — key passes
Passes that led directly to a shot, whether or not it went in. Raw creativity volume, where xA measures the quality of what was created.
xGChain (xGCh)
Credit for being anywhere in a move that ended in a shot: the full xG of the chance is credited to every player who touched the ball in the build-up. Rewards involvement, not just the final pass or shot.
xGBuildup (xGB)
xGChain minus the shot and the assist pass. What's left is pure build-up play — deep-lying passers and defenders who start attacks score high here even with zero goals and assists.
PPDA — pressing intensity
Opponent passes allowed per defensive action in their half. Counter-intuitive direction: a LOW number means an aggressive press (the opponent barely gets 10 passes before being tackled), a high number means the team sits back.
Deep completions
Passes received within roughly 20 metres of the opponent's goal (crosses excluded). A good measure of sustained territory and box presence.
xPts — expected points
How many points a match “should” have given based on both teams' chances: the chance quality is converted into win/draw/loss probabilities and summed over the season. A team far above its xPts has been winning tight or lucky games.
npxGD — underlying dominance
Non-penalty xG created minus non-penalty xG conceded, per match. The single best summary of how well a team actually played, ignoring finishing luck at both ends.

xG table — results vs expected

How to read this

What it shows. Results next to what the chances say they should have been. xG (expected goals) values every shot by how often that kind of chance is scored; xPts converts each match's shots into win/draw/loss probabilities and sums the expected points.

How to read it. Pts−xPts above zero means the team banked more points than its chances deserved — running hot on finishing, goalkeeping or timing. npxGD is non-penalty xG difference (created minus conceded), widely considered the best single number for underlying strength: it predicts future results better than points do.

Caveat. xG is a model of chance quality, not truth — elite finishers beat it consistently, and one season is a small sample.

Team comparison

How to read this

What it shows. Up to three teams overlaid on a radar of six style dimensions, each expressed as the team's percentile among the sides in that league. Attack is non-penalty xG created per match and Defence is non-penalty xG conceded (flipped, so further out = fewer chances allowed). Finishing is goals minus xG — conversion above or below what the chances deserved. Pressing is PPDA flipped (opponent passes allowed per defensive action — fewer means a higher press). Territory is deep completions per match (passes received within ~20m of the opponent goal) and Box defence is the same thing conceded, flipped.

How to read it. The shape is the identity: a dominant pressing side bulges toward Attack–Pressing–Territory, a low-block counter team can look small here yet still win points on Finishing and Box defence. The table underneath gives the raw per-match numbers behind each axis, plus points vs expected points. Shots on target aren't in the data — Understat's team feed doesn't publish them — so chance quality (xG) stands in for shot accuracy.

Head-to-head mode. With exactly two teams picked, the card goes deeper: a tale-of-the-tape strip where each bar is split by the two sides' league-percentile share (the longer half leads, the number in brackets is the league rank as a percentile ordinal), this season's actual meetings between the clubs with the score and each side's xG that day, the last five results, points and non-penalty xG difference split by home/away, and both teams' rolling 6-match npxGD form curves overlaid on one chart — so you can see not just who is better on the season, but who is better right now, and what happened when they actually met.

Pick two or three teams above to see their playing styles side by side. Picking exactly two unlocks a head-to-head deep dive: this season's meetings, a tale-of-the-tape bar duel, recent form, home/away splits and overlaid form curves.

Team style — pressing vs territory

How to read this

What it shows. Each team's playing identity in two numbers, averaged over the season. PPDA (passes per defensive action) counts how many passes a team lets the opponent play before making a tackle, interception or foul — fewer means a more aggressive press. Deep completions are passes completed within roughly 20 metres of the opponent's goal — a proxy for sustained territorial dominance.

How to read it. Top-left teams press high and pin opponents into their own box — the modern dominant style. Bottom-right teams sit deep and play direct, ceding the ball and the territory. Neither corner is 'better' — it's a style map, not a quality ranking. Hover a dot for exact values.

Form curves — rolling xG difference

How to read this

What it shows. Every team's underlying form across the whole season: non-penalty xG difference (chances created minus chances conceded, penalties excluded) averaged over a rolling 5-match window. Teams appear in final-table order and all curves share the same scale (±2.1), so shapes are directly comparable.

How to read it. Green stretches above the line are periods of outplaying opponents; red dips below are periods of being outplayed. Look for the story in the shape: a title challenge that faded after mid-season, a slow starter that clicked after a coaching change, a relegated team that was actually improving. The number after each name is the latest value; hover a curve for its season range.

One panel per team, final-table order · each runs matchday 5 → 38, the faint vertical line is mid-season · green above zero = out-creating opponents, red below = out-created · all panels share the same ±2.1 scale · one dot per matchday (hover for its value), the big dot and number = latest 5-match window

1 AC Milan+1.85

2 Inter+2.09

3 Napoli+0.49

4 Juventus−0.07

5 Lazio+0.45

6 Roma+0.40

7 Fiorentina−0.32

8 Atalanta+0.76

9 Verona−0.46

10 Sassuolo−0.83

11 Torino+0.14

12 Udinese−0.05

13 Bologna−0.11

14 Empoli−1.79

15 Sampdoria−0.69

16 Spezia−0.68

17 Salernitana+0.39

18 Cagliari−0.45

19 Genoa−0.25

20 Venezia−1.32

Deep completions are passes completed within roughly 20 metres of the opponent's goal — a proxy for sustained territorial dominance.

How to read it. Top-left teams press high and pin opponents into their own box — the modern dominant style. Bottom-right teams sit deep and play direct, ceding the ball and the territory. Neither corner is 'better' — it's a style map, not a quality ranking. Hover a dot for exact values.

What it shows. Every team's underlying form across the whole season: non-penalty xG difference (chances created minus chances conceded, penalties excluded) averaged over a rolling 5-match window. Teams appear in final-table order and all curves share the same scale (±2.5), so shapes are directly comparable.

Look for the story in the shape: a title challenge that faded after mid-season, a slow starter that clicked after a coaching change, a relegated team that was actually improving. Players (2021/22, Understat)

Metric glossary — what xG, npxG, xGBuildup, PPDA and friends actually mean
xG — expected goals
How good a team's or player's chances were. Every shot gets a value between 0 and 1: the probability that an average player scores from that spot and situation. A tap-in is ~0.9, a hopeful 30-metre hit ~0.02. Add them up and you get how many goals the chances “should” have produced.
xA — expected assists
The same idea for passing: the xG of the shot a pass created. A player who keeps serving up big chances gets a high xA even if teammates keep missing them.
npxG / npg — non-penalty xG and goals
xG and goals with penalties removed. A penalty is worth ~0.76 xG no matter who wins it, so stripping them out shows how much a player or team creates from open play.
G−xG — finishing
Goals scored minus expected goals. Above zero: converting chances an average finisher would miss. Below zero: missing chances that usually go in. Tends to swing back toward zero over time.
KP — key passes
Passes that led directly to a shot, whether or not it went in. Raw creativity volume, where xA measures the quality of what was created.
xGChain (xGCh)
Credit for being anywhere in a move that ended in a shot: the full xG of the chance is credited to every player who touched the ball in the build-up. Rewards involvement, not just the final pass or shot.
xGBuildup (xGB)
xGChain minus the shot and the assist pass. What's left is pure build-up play — deep-lying passers and defenders who start attacks score high here even with zero goals and assists.
PPDA — pressing intensity
Opponent passes allowed per defensive action in their half. Counter-intuitive direction: a LOW number means an aggressive press (the opponent barely gets 10 passes before being tackled), a high number means the team sits back.
Deep completions
Passes received within roughly 20 metres of the opponent's goal (crosses excluded). A good measure of sustained territory and box presence.
xPts — expected points
How many points a match “should” have given based on both teams' chances: the chance quality is converted into win/draw/loss probabilities and summed over the season. A team far above its xPts has been winning tight or lucky games.
npxGD — underlying dominance
Non-penalty xG created minus non-penalty xG conceded, per match. The single best summary of how well a team actually played, ignoring finishing luck at both ends.

Player explorer

How to read this

What it shows. Every player Understat tracks in the selected league this season (2848 across the big five). The table starts with the top 25 by the current sort — use the buttons under it to load more. Search by name or club, filter by position and minutes, and click any column header to sort (click again to flip direction). Click a row to open that player's profile card, with season totals and percentile bars against players of the same position.

The columns. xG and xA are expected goals and expected assists — the value of the chances a player took or created. G−xG above zero means finishing better than the chances deserved; A−xA above zero means teammates converted the chances generously. KP is key passes (passes leading directly to a shot), npxG strips out penalties.

Per 90. The toggle converts volume stats to per-90-minute rates, which makes part-time players comparable to ever-presents — players under 270 minutes are hidden in that mode to avoid tiny-sample noise. Players transferred mid-season show both clubs, comma-separated.

Player comparison

How to read this

What it shows. Up to three players overlaid on a radar of six per-90 attacking dimensions — non-penalty expected goals (npxG: the value of the shots taken, penalties excluded), expected assists (xA: the value of the chances created), shots, key passes (passes leading to a shot), xGChain (involvement anywhere in a scoring move) and xGBuildup (build-up play only, shots and assists excluded — see the glossary at the top of this tab). Each axis is the player's percentile among players of the same position in their own league with 450+ minutes, so a defender isn't drowned by striker numbers; the table below gives the exact per-90 rates.

How to read it. The bigger the shape, the more complete the attacking contribution — but shape profile matters more than area: a pure finisher spikes toward npxG and shots, a creator toward xA and key passes, a deep engine toward xGBuildup. Comparing a striker with a full-back is fair here because each is measured against their own position group — and since the search spans all five leagues, cross-league match-ups work the same way: each player is ranked against their own league's peers, so the radar answers “who dominates their context more”, not who would outscore whom in the same league.

Pick two or three players above (or use “Add to comparison” on a player card) to see their profiles side by side. The search covers all five leagues, so cross-league match-ups work too.

Clinical finishers — most goals above xG

How to read this

What it shows. The players (≥900 minutes) whose goal tallies differ most from the value of their chances. G−xG is goals scored minus expected goals: far above zero means converting chances an average finisher would miss.

How to read it. A single hot season can be luck; players who beat their xG year after year are genuinely elite finishers. Check the shots column too — a big overshoot on few shots is far flukier than the same overshoot on a hundred.

PlayerTeamMinShotsGoalsxGG−xG
Dusan VlahovicFiorentina,Juventus29481192418.2+5.8
Dries MertensNapoli137745115.3+5.7
FabiánNapoli24153971.5+5.5
Giovanni SimeoneCagliari,Verona2690701712.0+5.0
PedroLazio20483694.1+4.9
Gianluca ScamaccaSassuolo2166791611.1+4.9
Gianluca CaprariVerona277767127.2+4.8
Ciro ImmobileLazio27311182722.4+4.6

Wasteful in front of goal — most goals below xG

How to read this

What it shows. The other end of the list — players (≥900 minutes) who scored the fewest goals relative to the chances they had.

How to read it. This is not simply a wall of shame: a player here with a high xG is still getting into great positions, which is the hard part — finishing tends to bounce back. A player with low xG and a big negative gap has a real problem.

Top creators by expected assists

How to read this

What it shows. The league's best chance creators (≥900 minutes), ranked by expected assists — the probability that the shots their passes created would be scored.

How to read it. xA measures the quality of the chance served, independent of whether the teammate buried it. A−xA below zero means the creator was let down by finishing; above zero means teammates converted generously. xA is the fairer ranking of creativity than raw assists.

Insights (2021/22, Understat)

Second-order reads of the xG data: what the raw tables hide.

Metric glossary — what xG, npxG, xGBuildup, PPDA and friends actually mean
xG — expected goals
How good a team's or player's chances were. Every shot gets a value between 0 and 1: the probability that an average player scores from that spot and situation. A tap-in is ~0.9, a hopeful 30-metre hit ~0.02. Add them up and you get how many goals the chances “should” have produced.
xA — expected assists
The same idea for passing: the xG of the shot a pass created. A player who keeps serving up big chances gets a high xA even if teammates keep missing them.
npxG / npg — non-penalty xG and goals
xG and goals with penalties removed. A penalty is worth ~0.76 xG no matter who wins it, so stripping them out shows how much a player or team creates from open play.
G−xG — finishing
Goals scored minus expected goals. Above zero: converting chances an average finisher would miss. Below zero: missing chances that usually go in. Tends to swing back toward zero over time.
KP — key passes
Passes that led directly to a shot, whether or not it went in. Raw creativity volume, where xA measures the quality of what was created.
xGChain (xGCh)
Credit for being anywhere in a move that ended in a shot: the full xG of the chance is credited to every player who touched the ball in the build-up. Rewards involvement, not just the final pass or shot.
xGBuildup (xGB)
xGChain minus the shot and the assist pass. What's left is pure build-up play — deep-lying passers and defenders who start attacks score high here even with zero goals and assists.
PPDA — pressing intensity
Opponent passes allowed per defensive action in their half. Counter-intuitive direction: a LOW number means an aggressive press (the opponent barely gets 10 passes before being tackled), a high number means the team sits back.
Deep completions
Passes received within roughly 20 metres of the opponent's goal (crosses excluded). A good measure of sustained territory and box presence.
xPts — expected points
How many points a match “should” have given based on both teams' chances: the chance quality is converted into win/draw/loss probabilities and summed over the season. A team far above its xPts has been winning tight or lucky games.
npxGD — underlying dominance
Non-penalty xG created minus non-penalty xG conceded, per match. The single best summary of how well a team actually played, ignoring finishing luck at both ends.

The justice table — where the chances say you belonged

How to read this

What it shows. The league re-ranked by expected points. xPts turns each match's chances into win/draw/loss probabilities and sums the expected points — so this is the table with finishing luck, deflections and goalkeeping heroics stripped out. Stripes mark where the Champions League and relegation places would have gone.

How to read it. ▲ in the Fortune column means the team finished that many places higher in the real table than its chances deserved — a fortunate season likely to regress. ▼ means the table undersold them; those teams are the classic bounce-back picks for next season, and where the value hides in pre-season betting markets and predictions.

xPts rankTeamxPtsActual ptsActual rankFortune
1Inter80.7842▼1
2AC Milan72.8861▲1
3Napoli72.0793=
4Roma68.2636▼2
5Juventus66.2704▲1
6Atalanta64.4598▼2
7Fiorentina61.6627=
8Lazio61.0645▲3
9Torino58.75011▼2
10Verona53.3539▲1
11Udinese52.74712▼1
12Sassuolo47.05010▲2
13Bologna43.94613=
14Genoa38.92819▼5
15Cagliari38.63018▼3
16Spezia38.03616=
17Sampdoria37.43615▲2
18Empoli36.74114▲4
19Salernitana34.53117▲2
20Venezia28.32720=

Where the luck lived — finishing vs goalkeeping

How to read this

What it shows. Every team's season luck, split into its two ingredients. The horizontal axis is finishing: goals scored minus the xG of the chances taken. The vertical axis is the defensive mirror: the xG of chances faced minus goals actually conceded — beating it means the keeper and defenders repelled more than the model expected.

How to read it. The dashed lines are 'exactly as expected'. A team deep in the top-right corner won its points with hot finishing and heroic goalkeeping at once — a combination that history says doesn't repeat. A bottom-left team was punished at both ends and is almost certainly better than its results. The interesting cases are the off-diagonal ones: a clinical attack can mask a genuinely leaky defence in the goal-difference column, and this chart un-masks it.

The chaos index — quality vs volatility

How to read this

What it shows. Two dimensions of a season that a league table can't separate: how good a team's underlying performance was (average non-penalty xG difference per match, horizontal) and how wildly it swung from week to week (its standard deviation, vertical).

How to read it. Bottom-right is the champion profile — dominant nearly every week, no drama. Top-right teams mix demolitions with inexplicable no-shows; they often underachieve their talent because football caps a rout at 3 points. Bottom-left teams are steadily, reliably outplayed. Top-left is the neutral's favourite: total chaos, capable of anything on any given Sunday. Volatility also hints at squad depth and tactical rigidity — thin squads and one-plan teams swing harder.

Venue dependence — who's a different team on the road

How to read this

What it shows. The 4 most home-dependent teams and the 4 most venue-proof ones — measured by underlying performance (non-penalty xG difference per match), not results. Results split by venue mix in luck; this measures how differently a team actually plays at home versus away.

How to read it. A big home edge suggests a style that needs its own conditions — the crowd's energy for a press, a familiar pitch for a passing game — and makes away fixtures against them far more winnable than the table implies. A near-zero or negative edge is genuinely rare and marks a mentally robust, system-driven side. Useful for match predictions: venue matters much more for some teams than others.

TeamHome npxGD/matchAway npxGD/matchHome edge
Fiorentina+0.97−0.36+1.33
Inter+1.68+0.69+0.99
Salernitana−0.40−1.16+0.76
Sampdoria−0.24−0.98+0.74
Napoli+0.57+0.44+0.13
AC Milan+0.81+0.79+0.02
Empoli−0.79−0.63−0.16
Atalanta+0.47+0.69−0.22

Shot diet — volume vs chance quality

How to read this

What it shows. The league's 30 highest-volume shooters (≥900 minutes) plotted by how often they shoot (horizontal) against the average quality of each attempt (vertical, npxG per shot — penalties excluded, since a spot-kick would poison the average).

How to read it. A shot worth 0.20 npxG is a one-in-five chance, close to goal; a 0.05 shot is a hopeful hit from distance. Top-left poachers shoot rarely but only from gold positions. Bottom-right 'chancers' rack up flashy shot counts that are worth little each — high highlight-reel value, low goal value. Top-right, high volume and high quality, is the elite-striker profile and the rarest spot on the chart. Hover a dot for exact numbers.

Hidden engines — buildup value without the headlines

How to read this

What it shows. When a move ends in a shot, xGChain credits the shot's xG to every player who touched the ball in that possession. xGBuildup is the same but excludes the shooter and the assister — leaving only the contribution that never appears in any goals or assists column. This table ranks players (≥1800 minutes) by xGBuildup per 90 minutes.

How to read it. These are the league's under-credited attack-builders — note how many are defenders and deep midfielders with almost no G+A. A player high here is the platform their team's attack stands on; sell them and the forwards' numbers mysteriously dry up. This is exactly the kind of signal scouting departments pay for, and it's invisible in a normal stats page. The G+A column is shown precisely to highlight the gap.

PlayerTeamPosMinxGBuildup/90xGChain/90G+A
Marcelo BrozovicInterM29700.700.763
Nicolò BarellaInterM29530.530.8815
Milan SkriniarInterD31500.510.523
Maxime LopezSassuoloM S29480.500.584
Hakan CalhanogluInterM S25500.500.6519
Stefan de VrijInterD S24130.490.500
Alessandro BastoniInterD S23380.490.514
FabiánNapoliM S24150.440.5411
Gianluca CaprariVeronaF M S27770.430.7519
Teun KoopmeinersAtalantaM S21260.430.635
Ivan PerisicInterD M28600.420.6915
Remo FreulerAtalantaM S23980.420.494

Penalty merchants — goal tallies with an asterisk

How to read this

What it shows. The players whose goal tallies lean most on penalties. Pen share is the fraction of their goals scored from the spot; pen xG is the expected-goals value of those kicks.

How to read it. A penalty is converted about 76% of the time regardless of who takes it, so a scoring record built on them says more about who holds the ball when the referee points to the spot than about who creates goals from open play. Strip the penalties before comparing raw tallies, judging a transfer fee, or paying up at a fantasy-football auction — and remember penalty duty can vanish overnight with a squad change.

PlayerTeamPen goalsTotal goalsPen sharePen xG
Lorenzo InsigneNapoli91182%9.9
Ciro ImmobileLazio72726%6.9
Domenico BerardiSassuolo61540%4.6
Domenico CriscitoGenoa66100%5.3
Dusan VlahovicFiorentina,Juventus52421%4.6
Andrea PinamontiEmpoli41331%3.0
Antonin BarakVerona41136%3.0
Lautaro MartínezInter32114%4.6

How to read this

What it shows. The league re-ranked by expected points. xPts turns each match's chances into win/draw/loss probabilities and sums the expected points — so this is the table with finishing luck, deflections and goalkeeping heroics stripped out. Stripes mark where the Champions League and relegation places would have gone.

How to read it. ▲ in the Fortune column means the team finished that many places higher in the real table than its chances deserved — a fortunate season likely to regress. ▼ means the table undersold them; those teams are the classic bounce-back picks for next season, and where the value hides in pre-season betting markets and predictions.

8Manchester United54.5586▲2
9Brighton54.2519=
10West Ham53.6567▲3
11Aston Villa46.24514▼3
12Leeds43.93817▼5
13Everton43.53916▼3
14Southampton43.44015▼1
15Leicester42.3528▲7
16Newcastle United41.54911▲5
17Wolverhampton Wanderers40.55110▲7
18Burnley38.13518=
19Watford35.52319=
20Norwich25.02220=

Where the luck lived — finishing vs goalkeeping

How to read this

What it shows. Every team's season luck, split into its two ingredients. The horizontal axis is finishing: goals scored minus the xG of the chances taken. The vertical axis is the defensive mirror: the xG of chances faced minus goals actually conceded — beating it means the keeper and defenders repelled more than the model expected.

How to read it. The dashed lines are 'exactly as expected'. A team deep in the top-right corner won its points with hot finishing and heroic goalkeeping at once — a combination that history says doesn't repeat. A bottom-left team was punished at both ends and is almost certainly better than its results. The interesting cases are the off-diagonal ones: a clinical attack can mask a genuinely leaky defence in the goal-difference column, and this chart un-masks it.

The chaos index — quality vs volatility

How to read this

What it shows. Two dimensions of a season that a league table can't separate: how good a team's underlying performance was (average non-penalty xG difference per match, horizontal) and how wildly it swung from week to week (its standard deviation, vertical).

How to read it. Bottom-right is the champion profile — dominant nearly every week, no drama. Top-right teams mix demolitions with inexplicable no-shows; they often underachieve their talent because football caps a rout at 3 points. Bottom-left teams are steadily, reliably outplayed. Top-left is the neutral's favourite: total chaos, capable of anything on any given Sunday. Volatility also hints at squad depth and tactical rigidity — thin squads and one-plan teams swing harder.

Venue dependence — who's a different team on the road

How to read this

What it shows. The 4 most home-dependent teams and the 4 most venue-proof ones — measured by underlying performance (non-penalty xG difference per match), not results. Results split by venue mix in luck; this measures how differently a team actually plays at home versus away.

How to read it. A big home edge suggests a style that needs its own conditions — the crowd's energy for a press, a familiar pitch for a passing game — and makes away fixtures against them far more winnable than the table implies. A near-zero or negative edge is genuinely rare and marks a mentally robust, system-driven side. Useful for match predictions: venue matters much more for some teams than others.

TeamHome npxGD/matchAway npxGD/matchHome edge
Arsenal+0.99−0.29+1.28
Southampton+0.03−1.02+1.05
Crystal Palace+0.63−0.40+1.02
Brentford+0.54−0.40+0.94
Manchester City+1.65+1.61+0.04
Wolverhampton Wanderers−0.37−0.29−0.08
Chelsea+0.75+0.92−0.17
Watford−0.74−0.54−0.20

Shot diet — volume vs chance quality

How to read this

What it shows. The league's 30 highest-volume shooters (≥900 minutes) plotted by how often they shoot (horizontal) against the average quality of each attempt (vertical, npxG per shot — penalties excluded, since a spot-kick would poison the average).

How to read it. A shot worth 0.20 npxG is a one-in-five chance, close to goal; a 0.05 shot is a hopeful hit from distance. Top-left poachers shoot rarely but only from gold positions. Bottom-right 'chancers' rack up flashy shot counts that are worth little each — high highlight-reel value, low goal value. Top-right, high volume and high quality, is the elite-striker profile and the rarest spot on the chart. Hover a dot for exact numbers.

Hidden engines — buildup value without the headlines

How to read this

What it shows. When a move ends in a shot, xGChain credits the shot's xG to every player who touched the ball in that possession. xGBuildup is the same but excludes the shooter and the assister — leaving only the contribution that never appears in any goals or assists column. This table ranks players (≥1800 minutes) by xGBuildup per 90 minutes.

How to read it. These are the league's under-credited attack-builders — note how many are defenders and deep midfielders with almost no G+A. A player high here is the platform their team's attack stands on; sell them and the forwards' numbers mysteriously dry up. This is exactly the kind of signal scouting departments pay for, and it's invisible in a normal stats page. The G+A column is shown precisely to highlight the gap.

PlayerTeamPosMinxGBuildup/90xGChain/90G+A
RodriManchester CityM29010.740.819
Thiago SilvaChelseaD S26380.610.623
Rúben DiasManchester CityD S24030.600.636
JorginhoChelseaM S22850.600.648
João CanceloManchester CityD32330.590.758
Reece JamesChelseaD M S18660.580.7714
Aymeric LaporteManchester CityD28480.580.594
Pierre-Emile HøjbjergTottenhamM32020.560.684
Jordan HendersonLiverpoolM S26160.530.657
Antonio RüdigerChelseaD30360.500.533
Andrew RobertsonLiverpoolD25500.500.6613
Virgil van DijkLiverpoolD30600.480.484

Penalty merchants — goal tallies with an asterisk

How to read this

What it shows. The players whose goal tallies lean most on penalties. Pen share is the fraction of their goals scored from the spot; pen xG is the expected-goals value of those kicks.

How to read it. A penalty is converted about 76% of the time regardless of who takes it, so a scoring record built on them says more about who holds the ball when the referee points to the spot than about who creates goals from open play. Strip the penalties before comparing raw tallies, judging a transfer fee, or paying up at a fantasy-football auction — and remember penalty duty can vanish overnight with a squad change.

PlayerTeamPen goalsTotal goalsPen sharePen xG
JorginhoChelsea66100%5.3
Mohamed SalahLiverpool52322%4.6
Wilfried ZahaCrystal Palace51436%5.3
Ivan ToneyBrentford51242%3.8
Harry KaneTottenham41724%3.0
RaphinhaLeeds41136%3.0
Riyad MahrezManchester City41136%3.8
James Ward-ProwseSouthampton41040%3.0

The justice table — where the chances say you belonged

How to read this

What it shows. The league re-ranked by expected points. xPts turns each match's chances into win/draw/loss probabilities and sums the expected points — so this is the table with finishing luck, deflections and goalkeeping heroics stripped out. Stripes mark where the Champions League and relegation places would have gone.

How to read it. ▲ in the Fortune column means the team finished that many places higher in the real table than its chances deserved — a fortunate season likely to regress. ▼ means the table undersold them; those teams are the classic bounce-back picks for next season, and where the value hides in pre-season betting markets and predictions.

xPts rankTeamxPtsActual ptsActual rankFortune
1Barcelona73.6732▼1
2Real Madrid73.6861▲1
3Atletico Madrid69.0713=
4Real Sociedad64.0626▼2
5Villarreal63.9597▼2
6Athletic Club62.8558▼2
7Sevilla57.0704▲3
8Real Betis56.5655▲3
9Levante52.73519▼10
10Celta Vigo51.24611▼1
11Rayo Vallecano50.54214▼3
12Osasuna49.04710▲2
13Valencia46.3489▲4
14Cadiz44.23915▼1
15Getafe42.93916▼1
16Espanyol40.04213▲3
17Mallorca39.83917=
18Alaves37.63120▼2
19Granada37.23818▲1
20Elche35.74212▲8

The vertical axis is the defensive mirror: the xG of chances faced minus goals actually conceded — beating it means the keeper and defenders repelled more than the model expected.

How to read it. The dashed lines are 'exactly as expected'. A team deep in the top-right corner won its points with hot finishing and heroic goalkeeping at once — a combination that history says doesn't repeat. A bottom-left team was punished at both ends and is almost certainly better than its results. Top-right teams mix demolitions with inexplicable no-shows; they often underachieve their talent because football caps a rout at 3 points. Bottom-left teams are steadily, reliably outplayed. Venue dependence — who's a different team on the road

How to read this

What it shows. The 4 most home-dependent teams and the 4 most venue-proof ones — measured by underlying performance (non-penalty xG difference per match), not results. Results split by venue mix in luck; this measures how differently a team actually plays at home versus away.

How to read it. A big home edge suggests a style that needs its own conditions — the crowd's energy for a press, a familiar pitch for a passing game — and makes away fixtures against them far more winnable than the table implies. A player high here is the platform their team's attack stands on; sell them and the forwards' numbers mysteriously dry up. Best of Europe (2021/22, Understat)

Continental leaderboards: the five leagues' players and teams in one view.

Read this first. This tab pours five very different leagues into one pot — and that comparison is fundamentally flawed. The xG model prices a chance the same everywhere, but the leagues differ wildly in pace, defensive quality, tactical style and squad depth: 0.8 npxG+xA per 90 against Ligue 1 defences is not the same achievement as the same number in the Premier League, and no cross-league adjustment is applied here.

Treat these boards as a fun conversation starter, not a verdict.

Most dangerous attackers in Europe

How to read this

What it shows. The 25 most dangerous attackers across all five leagues, ranked by npxG+xA per 90 — the value of the shots a player takes plus the chances they create, penalties excluded, per full match played. Only players with 1350+ minutes (roughly 15 full matches) qualify, so small-sample super-subs don't flood the list. npG+A is the raw non-penalty goals plus assists actually banked.

How to read it. This is a threat ranking, not a talent ranking: it measures the danger a player generated in their own league (see the note at the top of this tab). It also leans attacking by construction — deep playmakers and defenders live in the xGBuildup column of the player explorer, not here.

Continental justice table — xPts per match

How to read this

What it shows. The continental justice table: the 20 strongest teams in Europe by expected points per match, i.e. what each team's chances deserved. Everything is per match because the Bundesliga and Ligue 1 play 34 games while the other three play 38 — season totals would flatter the longer leagues.

How to read it. npxGD/m (non-penalty xG difference per match) is the best single strength number; Pts−xPts/m above zero means the team banked more than its chances deserved. And once more: each team earned these numbers against its own league's opposition, so this ranks domestic dominance, not head-to-head strength.
